student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_desc: Everyday phrasal verbs for daily routines and social navigation -- get up, run into, look after, turn down, sort out and more, separable and inseparable -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Phrasal Verbs in Real Life
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 14 -- Phrasal Verbs in Real Life

vocab.title: Match each phrasal verb to its meaning
defs: to leave your bed after you wake up
to take care of someone or something
to stop trying to do something
to say no to an offer or invitation
to complete a form with your information
to take someone or something to a place and leave it
to go and collect someone or something
to solve a problem or put things in order
to move something to a later time
to have a friendly relationship with someone

reading.rtitle: A Day in My New Life
paras: On a normal weekday, I wake up at six o'clock, but I do not get up right away. I stay in bed for a few minutes and think about my day. When I finally get up, I make coffee and drop off my daughter at school before work. Some mornings are chaotic, and I have to sort out a dozen small problems before I even leave the house.

Life in a new country means endless paperwork. Last week I had to fill out three long forms for the hospital, and in the middle of the second one I almost gave up. But I do not give up easily. On my way home, I ran into a neighbor at the pharmacy, and we chatted for twenty minutes. I get along really well with the people here, and a small, friendly conversation can turn a hard day around.

In the evening, I pick up my daughter from her friend's house and look after her until she falls asleep. Sometimes a colleague invites me out, and when I am too tired, I turn down the invitation politely. I try not to put off my rest, because a calm night at home is exactly what helps me wake up ready for the next day.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, a learner describes an ordinary day full of everyday phrasal verbs

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: It is a step-by-step guide on how to fill out hospital forms correctly.
Ana Paula describes an ordinary day and how small routines and social moments help her manage life in a new country.
It explains why living in a new country is impossible and not worth the effort.

parts: off my daughter at school. At the hospital, I often have to

gapfill.text: Phrasal verbs are a verb plus a small word (up, out, off, into). Together they get a new meaning: 'pick up' is not the same as 'pick'. Some are separable, so an object can go in the middle (I picked my daughter up / I picked her up). Some are inseparable, so the words stay together (I ran into a friend, never 'I ran a friend into'). A pronoun (it, her, him) always goes in the middle of a separable verb: pick it up, not 'pick up it'.

bank.label: Useful sentences for your daily routine
items: I wake up early, but I do not get up right away.
I drop off my daughter at school every morning.
I had to fill out a lot of forms this week.
I ran into an old friend at the pharmacy.
I get along really well with my neighbors.
Sometimes I have to turn down an invitation.
I try not to put off my rest.
In the evening, I pick up my daughter and look after her.

lf.text: Notice the pattern: separable verbs let an object sit in the middle (I filled the form out / I filled it out). Inseparable verbs keep the words together (I ran into her, never 'I ran her into'). A pronoun always goes in the middle of a separable verb: pick it up, not 'pick up it'.

items.situation: What time do you usually get up on a workday, and do you get up right away?
tips: I wake up at ..., but I do not get up right away.

items.situation: Tell me about a time you ran into someone you knew in an unexpected place.
tips: One day I ran into ... at ...
I once ran into an old friend while I was ...

items.situation: What is one task you keep putting off, and why?
tips: I always put off ..., but I should do it.

items.situation: Who do you look after, and what do you do for them?
tips: In the evening, I look after ... and I ...

items.situation: When did you last have to fill out a long form? How did it go?
tips: Last week I had to fill out ...
I filled out ..., and I almost gave up, but ...

items: Walk me through your morning. Use at least three phrasal verbs: 'I wake up ...', 'I get up ...', 'I drop off ...'.
Tell me about running into someone and about an invitation you turned down. Use 'I ran into ...' and 'I turned down ...'.
Describe a whole day, morning to night, with no notes. Mix separable and inseparable phrasal verbs.
A friend asks: 'What do you always put off, and what do you never give up on?' Answer with a full short story.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model answer
list: Model: 'I wake up at six, but I do not get up right away. After I get up, I drop off my daughter at school. At work I fill out forms and sort out small problems, and I never give up. On my way home, I sometimes run into a neighbor, and I get along well with everyone. In the evening, I pick up my daughter and look after her, and if I am tired, I turn down invitations so I do not put off my rest.'
Separable: fill out, drop off, pick up, sort out, put off, turn down, give up. Object can go in the middle (I filled it out).
Inseparable: run into, look after, get along (with). The words stay together (I ran into her).
A pronoun always goes in the middle of a separable verb: pick it up, drop her off, turn it down.
answerkey.note: Everyday phrasal verbs make your English sound natural. Learn the meaning as a whole, and remember which ones are separable and which are inseparable.

listenings.text: Most people think my mornings are calm, but the truth is they are a little crazy. I wake up at half past five, and honestly, I do not get up right away; I lie in bed and check the time twice. When I finally get up, I make breakfast and drop off my son at school on the way to the office. At work, the first thing I do is sort out my emails and fill out the reports from the day before. Some days I want to give up before nine o'clock, but I never do. The best part of my morning is small: I almost always run into the same friendly barista at the coffee shop, and that little chat sets up my whole day. A good routine is not about doing everything perfectly; it is about not putting off the things that matter.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I am calling because my week has been completely full, and I had to tell someone. On Monday, I had to fill out a huge stack of forms for my new apartment, and I nearly gave up halfway through. Then, on Tuesday, I ran into our old classmate Lucas at the supermarket, of all places. We get along so well that we talked for almost an hour! The rest of the week, I have been looking after my little niece while my sister is away, so I pick her up from daycare every afternoon. I actually turned down a dinner invitation last night because I was too tired. Anyway, I do not want to put off calling you any longer. Tell me about your week, and let us sort out a day to meet. Talk soon, bye!

extra_audio.text: On a busy morning, I wake up before my alarm. I get up, make coffee, and drop off my daughter at school. At work, I fill out forms and sort out small problems. On my way home, I run into a friend at the store. In the evening, I pick up my daughter and look after her.
